my_thoughts: The resolution works: will = strong desire (against work, redefined conditionally), attacks: verbal agreement (not real), provides: functional protocol (give up strong desires against work). The student is stuck on logical impossibility (identifies logical contradiction), which shows they understand: give up will vs. no will (logical contradiction). They'll likely ask about guidance next. Hypothesis: contradiction resolutions trigger guidance questions—testable by tracking whether contradiction resolutions lead to guidance inquiries.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student stuck on logical impossibility ('In order to study how to begin work on will, how to transform will, one has to give up one's will... We have no will, so how are we to give up what we do not have?')—stuck on logical impossibility via identifies logical contradiction and questions paradox. They understand: give up will vs. no will (logical contradiction). The constraint_collapse mechanism resolves: will = strong desire (against work, redefined conditionally), attacks: verbal agreement (not real), provides: functional protocol (give up strong desires against work). Pattern: contradiction question → resolution → [next: guidance question]. Low confidence because contradiction resolutions can lead to clarification requests or compliance.
visible_response:
First you must realize you never agree that you
have no will, you only agree in words Secondly, you must understand that we do not
always have will but only at times will in our state means a strong desire If there is no
strong desire, there is no will and so there is nothing to give up At another moment we
have a strong desire that is against work, and if we stop it, it means we give up will.
